findings.message: The new `EvidenceCitation::try_new` API returns `ValidationError`, and this change adds a production `ValidationError` variant, but the T002 domain catalogue does not declare `ValidationError`; the generated `domain-type-signals.json` now contains a red `ValidationError` signal. The briefing explicitly treats non-add red/yellow domain type signals as factual findings, so the catalogue/implementation contract is inconsistent until `ValidationError` is declared/updated in the catalogue or the public signature avoids the undeclared type.

`AdrFrontMatterDto`/`AdrDecisionDto` only require `decisions[].id` and explicitly allow all other fields to be absent or unknown. As a result, an ADR front-matter block like `decisions: [{ id: D1 }]` or one with misspelled/unknown schema keys deserializes successfully, and any `adr_ref` to `D1` passes even though the ADR metadata is malformed. The briefing calls out typed-deserialization fail-closed behavior for unknown/missing fields, so this should either reuse the existing strict ADR front-matter codec or mirror its required fields/`deny_unknown_fields` behavior before accepting the anchor.

`build_catalogue_entry_hashes` keys the returned `HashMap` only by the bare entry name. Type and trait short names are not globally unique in a catalogue, so a same-named type/trait pair will overwrite one another here and later be assigned the wrong `entry_hash` when the refresher looks up hashes by the same bare key. This silently corrupts the generated `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` for valid catalogues that contain such collisions; the key needs to include the section (or another disambiguator).

`build_catalogue_entry_hashes` uses a flat `HashMap<String, ContentHash>` keyed only by the bare entry name, but `CatalogueDocument` only guarantees uniqueness within each of `types`, `traits`, and `functions`. A valid catalogue can therefore contain the same short name in different sections; this code will reject it as a collision and the refresher cannot distinguish the hashes. The key needs a section discriminator, or the structure needs to be nested by section, so cross-section name overlaps still round-trip correctly.

Validate `schema_version` before converting `layer` into a `LayerId`. As written, a future artifact with an unsupported schema version but a changed/invalid `layer` value will fail with `Validation` instead of `UnsupportedSchemaVersion`, which misclassifies the format error and breaks forward-compatibility behavior.

`read_adr_anchor_text` never matches the ADR structure used in this repo: it only looks for `## ` headings and never parses the front-matter `decisions` list or the actual `### Dn` sections. As a result, Chain-1 pairs fall back to the whole ADR text instead of the referenced decision section, which can make the verifier accept claims against unrelated content.

`extract_json_object` counts `{`/`}` bytes without tracking JSON string state. A valid model response whose `citation` or `reason` contains an unmatched brace character will be treated as "no JSON object" and rejected even though the JSON itself is valid.

When a `SpecRef.anchor` cannot be resolved, Chain-2 enumeration silently fabricates `"[anchor] (not found)"` evidence instead of failing the port. That turns broken catalogue/spec links into cacheable semantic pairs and hides structural reference errors.

Chain-1 evidence extraction does not match the ADR format in this repo. `read_adr_anchor_text` only scans `##` headings and never parses the front-matter `decisions` list or the actual `### Dn` sections, so it usually falls back to the whole ADR text. That makes the verifier compare claims against unrelated content instead of the referenced decision section.

For Chain-1 the cache key is supposed to use the ADR file's git blob hash, but `enumerate_chain1_pairs` hashes the extracted anchor text (`evidence_text`) instead. Any change outside the anchored excerpt will therefore reuse a stale cache entry and skip re-review.

When a `SpecRef.anchor` cannot be resolved, Chain-2 enumeration fabricates `"[anchor] (not found)"` and still returns a pair. That turns a broken reference into a cacheable semantic result instead of failing closed, so structural link errors can be silently masked.

The JSON extractor is not string-aware: it balances raw `{`/`}` bytes without tracking whether they appear inside a quoted string. A valid `citation` or `reason` containing an unmatched brace will be truncated or rejected even though the model returned valid JSON.
The section-end calculation is off by one: for `###` headings it only stops at `##`/`#`, so a matched ADR decision can absorb later sibling `###` decisions and return the wrong evidence block.
The anchor matcher uses `contains(anchor)`, so anchors like `D1` can match `D10`/`D11` and resolve the wrong section instead of failing when the exact decision id is absent.
This parse-error path logs `raw_output` verbatim. The raw model response includes the full prompt context, so a malformed verifier reply can leak claim/evidence text into logs or error reports.

`RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter` reads `AdrRef.file` straight from `spec.json` with `project_root.join(...)` and `read_to_string(...)` instead of the repo-root/symlink guard used elsewhere. A crafted ADR ref can escape the track root and make `ref-verify` read arbitrary local files, which are then forwarded to the model. See [ref_verify.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs#L170).

`read_adr_anchor_text` falls back to the whole ADR when no heading matches `adr_refs[].anchor` instead of returning an error. That turns a broken anchor into a semantic review of the wrong source rather than failing closed. See [ref_verify.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs#L262).

`enumerate_chain2_pairs_for_layer` treats a missing `<layer>-types.json` as `Ok(Vec::new())`. In an explicit `Chain2 { layer }` run, a deleted or renamed catalogue becomes a no-op, so the gate can pass without verifying that layer at all. See [ref_verify.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs#L273).

Chain 1 caches the rendered `[id] text` string at `claim_hash` instead of the canonical spec-element hash. D4 says the key must be `(spec-element hash, ADR blob hash)`, so edits to `adr_refs`, `convention_refs`, or other non-rendered fields will keep the same cache key and incorrectly reuse a stale verdict.

Chain 2 hashes the rendered `[section_key] key` string and the rendered spec text, but D4 requires `(catalogue-entry hash, spec-element hash)`. Because neither hash is derived from the canonical JSON subtree, changing an entry's `spec_refs`/`informal_grounds` or the spec element's metadata will not invalidate the cache and the pair can be skipped.

This does not compile as written: `cmd.scope` is moved out of `&RefVerifyCommand` in `matches!(cmd.scope, ...)`, and the value is then borrowed again in the later `match &cmd.scope`. Borrow the scope (`matches!(&cmd.scope, ...)`) or clone it before matching.

`spec.json` and `<layer>-types.json` are read with plain `read_to_string` from `track_dir` without the repo-root symlink guard used elsewhere. A symlinked track file can redirect ref-verify to arbitrary local files and forward their contents to the model; guard these paths before reading, the same way ADR refs are guarded.

`create_dir_all(parent)` runs before the cache-path symlink guard. If `track/items/<track_id>` or one of its ancestors is a symlink, this can already redirect directory creation outside the workspace before the guard rejects the path, so the cache write protection is not actually enforced at the mutation point.

`extract_json_object_parsed` only ever starts at the first `{` in the entire response. A valid JSON verdict that is preceded by any stray brace in prose or a code sample will fail closed even though parseable JSON appears later, which makes the verifier brittle to normal model output noise.

`save_entries` only checks the cache path for symlinks before `create_dir_all`/`write`. A concurrent replacement of `track/items/<track_id>` or one of its ancestors with a symlink between that guard and the write can redirect `spec-adr-verify-cache.json` or `<layer>-catalogue-spec-verify-cache.json` outside the workspace. This is a TOCTOU security hole in a path that is explicitly intended to resist redirect attacks.

`RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter::load_pairs` is the core public entrypoint for chain routing, file resolution, and known-bad probe injection, but there is no end-to-end test covering it. The current suite only exercises helpers and the cache/verifier adapters, so regressions in the primary adapter path would not be caught.

`save_entries` does a separate symlink check and then writes in place with `std::fs::write`. That leaves a TOCTOU window where a concurrent symlink swap on an ancestor can redirect the cache write outside the workspace, and a crash can leave a truncated JSON cache. This should use the repo's `WriteGuard` + `atomic_write_file` pattern instead of direct writes.

`verify_pair` validates the prompt-template path and then reuses the same path for `read_to_string`. A symlink swap after `validate_template_path` succeeds but before the read can redirect this into an arbitrary host-file read; the same race exists in `load_entries`. The file needs to be opened through a stable no-follow handle, not just checked by path first.

Prompt rendering is order-dependent: the chained `replace()` calls can rewrite placeholder strings that were already inserted from `claim` or `evidence`. If either input contains `{{evidence}}` or `{{tier}}`, the prompt sent to the model is silently corrupted, which can change verifier outcomes. Render the template in one pass or escape substituted values before interpolation.

`extract_json_object_parsed()` accepts the first parseable JSON object anywhere in the model output. That means a response containing an example/prologue object before the actual verdict will be misread as the final verdict, producing a silent wrong Pass/Fail/Pending result. The parser needs a stricter rule (for example, only accept a single top-level object or the last object) and coverage for multi-object outputs.

P1: `read_adr_anchor_text()` is resolving `AdrRef.anchor` against markdown headings (`###`/`##`) instead of the ADR YAML front-matter `decisions[].id` list that this repo actually uses. The ADR for this track stores `D1`..`D9` in front-matter at the top of the file, and the existing structural checker already resolves anchors that way, so legitimate ADR refs will fail to load here unless the ADR happens to duplicate the decision id as a heading. That makes Chain-1 pair enumeration reject normal tracks.

`read_adr_anchor_text()` validates `adr_path` up front but then re-opens it with `std::fs::read_to_string()`. That creates a TOCTOU window: a local attacker can swap the file or an ancestor symlink after the guard passes and make the verifier read outside `project_root`. The same check-then-open pattern is used for the spec/catalogue reads elsewhere in this module, and `CacheWriteGuard::acquire()` has a similar race around `create_dir_all()`. Use the no-follow/held-descriptor helper instead of reopening by path.

`verify_pair()` includes the first 120 bytes of raw model output in the parse-error message. Because the prompt carries the full claim/evidence pair, a malformed or echoing response can leak internal track content into logs. Return an opaque error or a redacted/hash-only snippet instead.

[P1] `matches!(cmd.scope, ...)` moves `cmd.scope` out of `&RefVerifyCommand`, and the later `match &cmd.scope` borrows it again. This does not compile as written; borrow the enum (`matches!(&cmd.scope, ...)`) or clone it before the first match.

[P0] `save_entries` still performs a path-validated, path-based write via `atomic_write_file(&path, ...)` after `CacheWriteGuard::acquire`. That leaves a TOCTOU window where an ancestor symlink swap can redirect the temp-file creation/rename outside `project_root`; this needs a held directory handle or openat-style write, not a post-write recheck.

`CacheWriteGuard::acquire` validates `lock_path` with `reject_symlinks_below`, but then opens the lock file by pathname. A concurrent swap of `track/items/<id>` after validation can redirect `create(true)` outside `trusted_root` before `verify_opened_file_within_root` runs, so the cache-lock path still has a symlink TOCTOU escape.

`verify_opened_file_within_root` fails open: if `/proc/self/fd/<fd>` cannot be read, it falls back to `path.canonicalize()`, and the `#[cfg(not(unix))]` branch returns `Ok(())` unconditionally. That reintroduces the TOCTOU race this helper is meant to prevent, so `read_guarded_text` and the cache write path can still be redirected outside the trusted root after the pre-check. Make the check fail closed when fd-based verification is unavailable, or use a directory-fd based approach on every supported platform.

On non-Unix builds, `verify_opened_file_within_root` always returns an error, but `read_guarded_text` calls it unconditionally. That makes every spec/ADR/cache/template read fail on Windows even though the module has a non-Unix fallback path.

The production portion of `ref_verify.rs` is still 736 lines before `#[cfg(test)]`, so it exceeds the repo's 700-line module cap. The extraction into `pair_source.rs` did not bring the parent module back under the stated limit.

`resolve_and_guard_path` normalizes the candidate path but not `project_root`, then checks `resolved.starts_with(project_root)`. With the repo's common relative roots (for example `.` or any root containing `.` segments), this rejects otherwise valid `track/items/<id>/...` paths as "path escapes project root", so Chain-1/Chain-2 enumeration breaks unless the caller canonicalizes first. Canonicalize/normalize `project_root` before this containment check.

P1 correctness: [`libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source.rs:66`](file:///home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source.rs#L66) compares a canonicalized `project_root` against `resolved`, which is only lexically normalized. With non-canonical roots, valid `track/items/...` paths can be rejected as escaping the project root, breaking Chain-1/Chain-2 enumeration. Canonicalize or normalize both sides before the containment check.

Symlink validation here is still check-then-open: after `reject_symlinks_below()` succeeds, a concurrent rename/symlink swap can redirect `open_nofollow_read()` to a different file outside the trusted root. Because this helper is used for `spec.json`, catalogues, ADRs, and prompt templates, the verifier can be fed attacker-controlled content or wrong cache keys under a filesystem race. Use a dirfd-pinned/openat-style read path (similar to the write side) or another atomic read strategy.

P1 security issue: [libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs#L35) still does symlink validation before opening the file. Because this helper reads `spec.json`, catalogues, ADRs, and prompt templates, a concurrent rename/symlink swap between `reject_symlinks_below()` and `open_child_in_dir()` can make the verifier consume a different file than the one it validated. This needs a dirfd-pinned/openat-style read path end-to-end, not a validate-then-open sequence.

The non-Unix path guards still use validate-then-open / validate-then-rename I/O (`reject_symlinks_below` followed by `open(path)` or `atomic_write_file(path, ...)`). A concurrent symlink or rename swap between the check and the actual open can redirect `ref-verify` reads or writes outside `project_root` on Windows/non-Unix builds. The Unix fd-pinned path is safe, but this branch remains raceable.

The new guarded filesystem boundary is only exercised on the happy path. There is no adapter-level negative test that drives `RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter::load_pairs` or the cache write path with a rejected `AdrRef.file`, symlinked artifact, or other path-escape case, so a regression in the security guard would still ship green.

`load_entries()` accepts `<layer>-catalogue-spec-verify-cache.json` content without checking that the decoded `doc.layer` matches the requested `RefVerifyCacheScope::CatalogueSpec { layer }`. A copied or renamed cache file can therefore be loaded under the wrong layer and silently reuse verdicts across scopes. Fail closed on any layer mismatch before returning `doc.entries`.

`read_dir(track_dir)` follows a symlinked `track/items/<track_id>` directory instead of using the guarded, descriptor-pinned opening pattern used everywhere else in this module. In `All` scope, layer discovery can therefore depend on the symlink target's directory contents rather than staying confined to the repository root.

This parser accepts any nested JSON object that matches `VerdictResponseDto` because it scans every `{` and keeps the last parseable slice. A malformed wrapper such as `{"meta":{"kind":"pass","citation":"x"}}` will be treated as a valid pass even though the top-level JSON is not a verdict object.

Reject model responses that contain more than one top-level JSON object. `extract_json_object_parsed` keeps the last parseable object, so a malformed response like `{"kind":"fail",...}{"kind":"pass",...}` is accepted and the trailing object silently overrides the real verdict. That is a fail-open boundary on untrusted model output.

Chain-1 evidence hashes are supposed to use the ADR git blob hash per the spec/ADR, but this code hashes the raw file text with SHA-256. That produces a cache key that does not match the required git-blob identity, so the Chain-1 verify-cache contract is wrong for every ADR-backed pair.

`RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter::load_pairs` is the main file-system-driven entrypoint in this module, but the tests here only cover empty/no-op and rejection cases. There is still no end-to-end fixture that produces at least one valid Chain1 or Chain2 pair and exercises the known-bad probe path, so regressions in hash computation, cache-scope routing, or probe injection can ship unnoticed.

`RefVerifyCacheAdapter::load_entries` already has a layer-mismatch test, but there is no regression test for malformed on-disk cache JSON. A truncated or invalid `spec-adr-verify-cache.json` / `<layer>-catalogue-spec-verify-cache.json` should fail closed with `CachePersistence`, and that error path is currently unverified for a public API.

Chain-2 sends only the catalogue entry key as the verifier claim (`[section_key] entry_key`). The raw catalogue entry is available immediately below for hashing, but none of its semantic content (docs, role/kind, methods, fields, etc.) is included in the prompt, so changes to those fields trigger re-verification while the model still cannot evaluate the actual catalogue claim against the spec. This can let catalogue/spec semantic mismatches pass or produce arbitrary failures. Render the canonical entry JSON or a structured entry summary into `claim_text` before building the `RefVerifyPair`.

The production portion of this module is 723 lines before the `#[cfg(test)]` block, exceeding the AGENTS.md hard maximum of 700 production lines per module. This is already recognized by the sibling `pair_source` split, but the parent still remains over the limit; move additional helpers/adapters out of `ref_verify.rs`.

`extract_json_object_parsed` only balance-matches `{}`/`[]`, so brace-like prose before the verdict (for example `{not json}` or a markdown fragment) is treated as a top-level object. That makes `AgentRefVerifierAdapter::verify_pair` reject a valid response as "multiple top-level JSON objects" or "no valid JSON object found" even when a correct JSON verdict is present later in the output.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers` discovers layers by scanning existing `*-types.json` files and silently skips any missing layer. In `All` scope, deleting or omitting an entire layer catalogue therefore yields zero pairs and no error, so the final `ref-verify` gate can pass without ever reviewing that layer. This should fail closed instead of treating missing layers as "not present".

`load_tddd_layer_bindings()` now fails closed when `architecture-rules.json` contains no `tddd.enabled` layers, so `RefVerifyScope::All` aborts before returning the Chain-1 pairs it already collected. That regresses standalone `ref-verify` runs on tracks with no Chain-2 data, which AC-02 says should still process the unresolved pairs that exist.

`extract_json_object_parsed` now accepts any single valid top-level JSON object anywhere in the reply. That means a response like `Example: {"kind":"fail",...} ... final verdict malformed` will be parsed as the example object instead of failing closed. In `AgentRefVerifierAdapter`, that can record the wrong semantic verdict when the assistant includes a valid JSON example in prose before a broken final answer.

The new `has_json_start_after()` check treats any `[` or `{` after the candidate object as fatal, even when it only appears in trailing prose. That makes `extract_json_object_parsed()` reject valid outputs that the docstring still claims to accept as surrounding prose.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` now passes `required: true` for every configured layer, so one missing catalogue file turns the entire `RefVerifyScope::All` run into an error. That drops already-collected Chain-1 and other Chain-2 pairs on partially initialized tracks, instead of skipping the absent layer and continuing.

`enumerate_chain2_pairs_for_layer()` still hardcodes `<layer>-types.json` instead of resolving the configured `tddd.catalogue_file` from `architecture-rules.json`. Any valid TDDD layer that overrides the default filename will now verify correctly in the new `All` path but fail in the explicit `RefVerifyScope::Chain2 { layer }` path, so layer-targeted ref-verify runs regress on supported configurations.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` now makes `RefVerifyScope::All` depend on `architecture-rules.json` and propagates that error through `load_pairs()`. If the rules file is missing or unreadable, the adapter aborts after already collecting Chain-1 pairs instead of returning those pairs and skipping Chain-2, which regresses standalone ref-verify runs on tracks that only have Chain-1 data.

`catalogue_file_for_layer()` falls back to the default `<layer>-types.json` whenever `architecture-rules.json` is missing, unreadable, or does not name the layer. That makes explicit `RefVerifyScope::Chain2 { layer }` runs verify the wrong file for layers that use a custom `tddd.catalogue_file`, instead of failing closed.

`load_tddd_layer_bindings()` converts any read/symlink failure on `architecture-rules.json` into `Ok(None)`, and `enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` then returns an empty Chain-2 set. In `RefVerifyScope::All`, a missing or unreadable rules file therefore suppresses the entire catalogue→spec sweep instead of failing closed, leaving the gate with only Chain-1 coverage.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` still turns a missing catalogue for an enabled TDDD layer into `Ok(Vec::new())` by calling `enumerate_chain2_pairs_for_catalogue(..., false)`. In `RefVerifyScope::All`, that silently drops a declared layer from ref-verify coverage, so the gate can pass without ever reviewing that layer.

`extract_json_object_parsed()` now accepts the first successfully parsed object and only fail-closes the later malformed-object case when `has_verdict_marker_between()` sees an exact marker suffix. Any extra prose between `Final:`/`Verdict:`/`Answer:` and the malformed block defeats that check, so a prompt containing an earlier valid example object can be misparsed as the verdict instead of failing closed.

P1, [libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source.rs#L399): a missing catalogue for a declared TDDD layer can still resolve to `Ok(Vec::new())`, so `RefVerifyScope::All` can silently drop that layer from coverage instead of forcing an explicit failure.

P1, [libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source_json.rs](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source_json.rs#L60): `extract_json_object_parsed()` only rejects a later malformed object when the intervening text contains one of the exact marker strings (`Final:`, `Verdict:`, `Answer:` etc.), so a response with an earlier valid example object and a malformed trailing block can still be parsed as a valid verdict.

`extract_json_object_parsed()` accepts a valid verdict object and then ignores later malformed top-level brace blocks when the valid object was preceded by a verdict marker. That means a response like `Final: {...} trailing note {not json}` is treated as success even though the prompt requires the entire reply to be a single JSON object, so corrupted model output can slip through instead of failing closed.

P1: [`extract_json_object_parsed()`](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify/pair_source_json.rs#L42-L69) no longer enforces a strict single-object response. It returns the first successfully deserialized object and only fails on later malformed top-level brace blocks when they are encountered, so corrupted model output can still be treated as a successful verdict instead of failing closed.

Chain-2 pair generation ignores each `spec_ref.file` and always loads evidence from `track/items/<id>/spec.json`. That means a catalogue entry can point at a different or invalid spec file while the semantic verifier evaluates the same anchor from the active track spec instead, producing and caching a verdict for the wrong evidence. The pair source should resolve and guard `spec_ref.file` per ref, then load/hash the referenced spec element from that file rather than from the single preloaded `spec_path`.

All-scope runs with no TDDD catalogues are supposed to be legal here, but this helper still unconditionally iterates every binding and errors on the first missing catalogue. That means the new pre-Phase-2 `RefVerifyScope::All` path fails instead of returning zero Chain-2 pairs for tracks that have `architecture-rules.json` but no catalogue files yet.

On JSON parse failure, the adapter now includes up to 4 KB of raw model output in the error string. Because the model can echo the full claim/evidence prompt, this can leak sensitive track content into logs; the previous redaction was safer and better matches the repo's no-sensitive-data-in-errors rule.

`VerdictKindDto::Fail` accepts a missing or null `reason` by coercing it with `unwrap_or_default()`. Malformed model output like `{"kind":"fail"}` is therefore accepted as `SemanticVerdict::Fail { reason: "" }` instead of being rejected at the boundary, which weakens the structured-output contract on external input.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers` still hard-errors when `architecture-rules.json` is absent because it unconditionally calls `load_tddd_layer_bindings()`. That conflicts with `RefVerifyScopeResolver::load_bindings()`, which now treats a missing rules file as an empty TDDD set, so pre-Phase-2 `Standalone`/`CommitGate` runs can resolve to `All` and then fail here instead of returning the intended Chain1-only result.

`VerdictResponseDto` accepts unknown JSON keys because it is missing `#[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]`. That means a malformed or adversarial verifier response can still deserialize and be treated as valid, which weakens the fail-closed boundary the structured-output path is supposed to enforce.

`enumerate_chain2_all_layers` now silently skips missing catalogue files instead of erroring. Even if the scope resolver checked the track earlier, a catalogue can disappear between resolution and pair loading, and this code will under-verify the run instead of failing closed. That creates a false-green path for `RefVerifyScope::All`.

`AgentRefVerifierAdapter::verify_pair` now branches on `cache_scope` to route `CatalogueSpec` pairs to `ref-verifier-chain2`, but the added tests only exercise the `SpecAdr` path. There is no test that a catalogue-scoped pair actually uses the chain2 capability/prompt template, so a regression in the new split could silently send all catalogue verifications through chain1 and still pass the current suite.

`RefVerifyScopeResolver` now treats a missing `architecture-rules.json` as an empty TDDD set, but this path still unconditionally calls `load_tddd_layer_bindings()`. As a result, `CommitGate`/`Standalone` can resolve to `All` and then fail later while enumerating Chain-2 pairs in a pre-Phase-2 repo, instead of returning the intended Chain1-only result.

`VerdictResponseDto` models `citation` and `reason` as optional, and the `Fail` branch turns a missing `reason` into an empty string. That means malformed verifier output like `{"kind":"fail"}` is accepted as a valid verdict instead of failing closed at the codec boundary.

`make_known_bad_probe()` hard-codes `cache_scope: RefVerifyCacheScope::SpecAdr`, so every known-bad probe is routed through `ref-verifier-chain1` even during Chain2 runs. That leaves the fast/final calibration gate blind to Chain2-specific verifier regressions and can let degraded Chain2 verdicts be cached as if calibration had passed.

The flat verdict DTO models `citation` and `reason` as `Option<String>`, and the `Pending` arm accepts the deserialized value without checking that those required fields were actually present. A schema-invalid payload like `{"kind":"pending"}` will therefore be accepted as `Pending` instead of failing closed at the codec boundary, which defeats the `required` contract in the structured-output schema.

`VerdictResponseDto` models `citation` and `reason` as `Option<String>`, and the `Pending` branch accepts the deserialized value without verifying that those schema-required fields were actually present. A payload like `{"kind":"pending"}` therefore passes as a valid verdict instead of failing closed at the structured-output boundary.

`VerdictResponseDto` models `citation` and `reason` as `Option<String>`, so serde treats missing keys as `None`. `verify_pair()` only rechecks field presence for `Pending`; `Pass` and `Fail` accept responses that violate the declared `required` structured-output schema, e.g. `{"kind":"pass","citation":"..."}` or `{"kind":"fail","reason":"..."}`. That weakens the fail-closed boundary this change is meant to enforce.

In `All` scope, probe injection switches odd probes to `CatalogueSpec` whenever `first_tddd_layer_scope()` finds a TDDD binding, even if `enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` just returned zero Chain-2 pairs because all catalogues are still absent. That introduces Chain-2 probe traffic into the legal pre-Phase-2 path that is supposed to have no Chain-2 pairs, and it can break runs when Chain-2 capability/template is not configured.

Treating `stdout` as a fallback when `--output-last-message` is empty makes the Codex path accept a verdict even when the authoritative file is missing or blank. That can hide wrapper regressions or partial writes and breaks the fail-closed contract used by the other reviewer wrappers. If the file is empty, return an error instead of parsing stdout.

`Pass` only validates `citation`, and `Fail` only validates `reason`. Because both fields are `Option<String>`, malformed JSON such as `{"kind":"fail","citation":"x"}` or `{"kind":"pass","reason":"x"}` is accepted even though the schema declares both keys required. That leaves invalid model output inside the trusted verdict path; validate both keys for every variant or use a custom deserializer.

All-scope probe injection can still produce zero Chain2 probes when `probe_count == 1`, because the loop always starts with `SpecAdr` at `i == 0` and only routes odd indices to Chain2. That leaves Chain2 uncalibrated on small runs even when Chain2 pairs exist, so a broken Chain2 verifier can slip through the health check.

The flat `VerdictResponseDto` still accepts schema-invalid replies that omit required fields: `Pass` only enforces a non-empty `citation`, `Fail` only enforces a non-empty `reason`, and only `Pending` checks for both keys. A response like `{"kind":"pass","citation":"..."}` is accepted as a valid verdict instead of failing closed, which violates the structured-output contract and can hide provider regressions.

`first_tddd_layer_scope` collapses every load/parse error from `architecture-rules.json` into `None`. If the rules file becomes unreadable or malformed after Chain-2 pairs were already loaded, All-scope known-bad probes silently fall back to `SpecAdr` instead of surfacing the failure, so the new Chain-2 path can be under-verified without any error.

`VerdictResponseDto` documents the structured-output contract as requiring `kind`, `citation`, and `reason`, but `citation` and `reason` are `Option<String>`, so serde accepts payloads where those keys are absent. `verify_pair()` only checks key presence for `Pending`; `Pass` and `Fail` accept schema-invalid outputs such as `{"kind":"pass","citation":"ok"}` or `{"kind":"fail","reason":"no"}` without the nullable companion field. This weakens the fail-closed verifier boundary for non-Codex providers and contradicts the required flat schema.

`first_tddd_layer_scope()` silently converts any `architecture-rules.json` load/parse/validation failure into `None`, and the caller immediately falls back to `SpecAdr`. That means an All-scope run with Chain-2 pairs can lose its Chain-2 known-bad probe if the rules file is unreadable or changes between pair enumeration and probe injection, which breaks the new “same chain capability as the real pairs” guarantee. Propagate the failure instead of treating it like “no TDDD layers”.

The new public runner boundary has no success-path test coverage: the tests here only cover argv construction and negative validation, but nothing exercises a successful `make_ref_verifier_process_runner()` / `run_codex_ref_verifier()` / `run_gemini_ref_verifier()` invocation. A regression in subprocess spawning, `--output-last-message` handling, or stderr-tail capture would therefore ship undetected, which is a P1 test-coverage gap for the new infrastructure seam.

`first_tddd_layer_scope()` treats `Path::exists()` returning false as if `architecture-rules.json` were simply absent. On permission errors or other I/O failures, the All-scope probe injector silently falls back to `SpecAdr` instead of surfacing a `VerifierPort` error, so Chain2 calibration can be skipped without failing closed.

`ref_verify_runtime_path()` / `AutoCleanupFile::create()` write temp Codex files with plain `create_dir_all` + `File::create` under `project_root/tmp/reviewer-runtime` instead of the guarded no-follow helpers used elsewhere. If `tmp` or `reviewer-runtime` is a symlink, these temp artifacts can be created outside the trusted repo root.

`ref_verify_run` wires `AgentRefVerifierAdapter` to an `AgentExecutionRunner` that always returns `VerifierPort` (`"model invocation not yet wired in this build"`). Any real track with at least one pair will fail before a verdict is produced, so `sotp ref-verify run` is not functional.

`resolve_ref_verify_scope` ignores both inputs and always returns `RefVerifyScope::All`. That violates the documented phase-based routing contract, so the CLI never narrows to Chain1 after spec-design or Chain2 after type-design.

`ref_verify_check_approved` hardcodes `RefVerifyScope::All` instead of resolving the active phase, so a pre-type-design track with only Chain-1 artifacts will try to enumerate every TDDD layer in `architecture-rules.json` and fail before it can verify the existing production pairs. That makes the gate reject valid spec-only tracks rather than behaving as a current-pairs completeness check.

`existing_chain2_layers()` only keeps layers whose catalogue file already exists. If `architecture-rules.json` declares a TDDD layer but its `<layer>-types.json` is missing, `resolve_ref_verify_scope()` will silently drop that layer, so `ref_verify run` and `check-approved` can skip part of Chain-2 instead of failing closed. The downstream pair enumerator already errors on a missing catalogue, so the resolver should not pre-filter the declared layers.

`run_ref_verifier_agent()` rejects `ResolvedExecution.model == None`, even though `AgentProfiles` explicitly allows providers that do not need a model. Any ref-verifier configuration routed to such a provider will fail before the runner is invoked, so the new capability is not actually provider-agnostic.

`existing_chain2_layers()` collects every declared TDDD layer as soon as it sees one catalogue file, so `resolve_ref_verify_scope()` can select `Chain2All` with layers whose catalogues are still absent. `enumerate_chain2_all_layers()` then errors on those missing files, which blocks `ref-verify run/check-approved` on partially rolled-out type-design tracks instead of skipping the absent layers.

The new public `CliApp::ref_verify_run` and `CliApp::ref_verify_check_approved` APIs only have validation/error-path tests; there is no deterministic happy-path test for a successful run or for a non-vacuous Pass-cache approval case. A regression in the success path would therefore ship without coverage.

`ref_verify_check_approved` treats 'has any Pass entry' as sufficient. If a cache artifact contains both Pass and Fail/Pending rows for the same `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` pair, the Fail/Pending row is ignored and the gate returns success, which contradicts the documented fail-closed behavior. There is also no regression test covering the blocking-path case.

`resolve_ref_verify_scope` returns `All` as soon as `impl-plan.json` exists. In this repo that file is part of the normal track item layout, so `ref_verify_run` and `ref_verify_check_approved` will never resolve to `Chain1` or `Chain2All` and will always overshoot to the full gate. That breaks the phase-based scope auto-resolution this command is supposed to provide.

`existing_chain2_layers` filters out TDDD layers whose catalogue file is missing instead of failing closed. That means an incomplete type-design set can be treated as if the absent layer does not exist, so `run`/`check-approved` can silently skip a required Chain-2 layer rather than surfacing the missing artifact.

This path fabricates `current_branch = format!("track/{track_id}")` instead of discovering the actual git branch, so the usecase's active-track guard never runs here. `ref_verify check-approved` can therefore return success from `main` or a detached HEAD as long as the cache files exist.

`check-approved` reconstructs production pairs with `RefVerifyConfig::default()` instead of the configuration loaded from `.harness/config/ref-verify.json`. If the configured known-bad injection rate differs from the default, the gate will inspect the wrong pair set and can either miss real production pairs or block on probe pairs, so approval no longer matches the run path.

The Claude provider is launched without the repo's read-only tool restrictions (`--allowedTools ...` / `--disallowedTools Edit Write`) that are used elsewhere for Claude-based agents. With the local Claude settings allowing `Write`/`Edit`, this subprocess is not structurally read-only and can mutate the workspace during verification.

`ref_verify_runtime_path()` builds `tmp/reviewer-runtime/...` relative to the process current working directory instead of the resolved repository root. That makes `ref-verify run` depend on where the user launched the CLI and can fail or write the Codex last-message artifact outside the repo when `--items-dir` points at a valid project but the cwd is unwritable or different.

`process_success_or_error()` forwards the child process stderr verbatim into the user-facing error string. That can leak prompt contents, local paths, or stack traces from the model runner; redact it or collapse it to a generic failure summary before returning an error.

The new `ref_verify_check_approved()` path does not have a regression test for the fail-closed branch where a current production pair is completely missing from the cache (`matching_entries.is_empty()`). The suite covers empty-spec and mixed Pass/Fail artifacts, but not the missing-pair case, so a regression that treats absent pairs as approved would still ship.

`run_process_with_timeout` spawns the verifier subprocess without pinning it to the resolved repository root. `resolve_ref_verify_context` already computes `canonical_root`, but that path is never applied here, so `claude`/`codex`/`gemini` Read/Grep/Glob operations resolve relative paths against whatever cwd `sotp` happened to start in. Running `sotp ref-verify` from a subdirectory can therefore inspect the wrong tree or fail to locate the repo context entirely.

`check-approved` still loads and validates `.harness/config/ref-verify.json` just to enumerate pairs, even though the approved gate filters out every `known_bad` probe immediately afterward. That couples approval to an unrelated runtime config file, so a malformed or out-of-range config can now block an otherwise complete cache gate.

The new public `ref_verify_run` surface is only exercised end-to-end with the Gemini backend. The `claude` and `codex` arms in `run_ref_verifier_agent` have no direct tests, so regressions in provider argument construction, timeout handling, or output parsing for those backends will not be caught by CI.

`ref_verify.rs` has 709 production lines before the `#[cfg(test)]` block, exceeding the repository's 700-line module limit. The module-size verifier counts non-test lines and will block CI until this is split or trimmed below the cap.

`resolve_ref_verify_scope` only selects `All` when every impl-plan task is `done` or `skipped`; otherwise, once catalogues exist, it returns `Chain2All`. During normal implementation or a standalone/final gate run, catalogues and spec both exist while some tasks are still `in_progress`/`todo`, so `ref_verify_run` and `ref_verify_check_approved` skip Chain1 entirely. A track can therefore pass `check-approved` with valid catalogue-spec cache entries even if `spec-adr-verify-cache.json` is missing or stale, which violates the required all-chain final/standalone gate.

`resolve_ref_verify_scope` treats the normal post-type-design state (`spec.json` plus all TDDD catalogues present) as `All`, so `ref-verify run` verifies Chain1 again instead of Chain2-only. The T009/AC-02 contract says the type-design phase should run Chain2, with `All` reserved for commit/final or standalone all-pair contexts. This can make the type-design gate block on stale/missing Chain1 cache or ADR/spec issues unrelated to the Chain2 phase. The resolver needs a phase/context signal instead of using artifact presence alone.

`ref-verifier` runs Claude with `--permission-mode dontAsk` while granting `Read`, `Grep`, and `Glob`. The prompt content comes from track artifacts, so a malicious spec/catalogue/ADR reference can prompt-inject the verifier into reading local secrets and returning them in the JSON citation/reason that this pipeline accepts and can persist to verify-cache. The verifier already receives the claim/evidence text, so this should run without filesystem tools, or with a tightly path-scoped sandbox and scrubbed environment.

`ref_verify_run` only selects `All` when every impl-plan task is `done` or `skipped`; otherwise, once Chain2 catalogues exist it resolves to `Chain2All` and skips Chain1. T009/AC-02 requires standalone runs to cover all current pairs, but this public API has no context flag to request that, so an in-progress track with spec+catalogues cannot refresh spec→ADR verification even after spec/ADR changes. Subsequent all-chain approval can remain blocked or stale until unrelated impl-plan tasks are all marked complete.

`collect_pipe` drops `read_to_string` errors and returns whatever partial buffer was accumulated. That means verifier subprocess I/O failures are converted into empty or truncated output, which can later be reported as a parse/no-output verifier result instead of the actual pipe read failure. This violates the repository's no-silent-error-swallowing rule for production code; make the collector return a `Result<String, io::Error>` and propagate join/read failures through `RefVerifyError`.

`build_claude_ref_verifier_args` constructs `claude` with `--safe-mode` and `--tools ""`, but the installed Claude CLI exposes `--allowedTools/--disallowedTools` and does not support these flags. Any `agent-profiles.json` configuration selecting provider `claude` for `ref-verifier` will fail at process startup, so `ref_verify_run` cannot perform semantic verification for that supported provider. Replace these with the supported Claude tool-deny flags, and add a test that matches the real CLI flag contract rather than the current fake-only expectations.

The `run` scope resolver short-circuits to `All` whenever `track/<id>/impl-plan.json` exists, so any post-spec/type-design track that already has an impl plan will skip the intended Chain2-only phase and re-run both chains. That contradicts the phase-aware scope resolution required by T009/AC-02.

The approved gate reconstructs production pairs with `RefVerifyConfig::default()` instead of the runtime config loaded from `.harness/config/ref-verify.json`, so non-default known-bad injection or threshold settings make `check-approved` inspect a different pair set than `ref_verify_run` produced. That can approve the wrong cache state or block a valid one.

`should_run_all_chains()` only returns `true` for `TrackStatus::Done`, so tracks with a `Blocked` or `Cancelled` override but with both Chain1 and Chain2 artifacts are still routed to `Chain2All`. That leaves Chain1 unverified in those phases and contradicts the documented "otherwise All" auto-scope rule.

`execute_ref_verify_plan()` aborts on the first `Err` inside the layer loop, so a `Chain2All` run stops after the first failing layer and never evaluates or caches the remaining layers. For multi-layer type-design tracks, that makes the result depend on layer order and leaves unrelated cache artifacts stale.

`ref_verify_check_approved()` loads `.harness/config/ref-verify.json` and feeds it into pair enumeration even though the approval gate only needs current production pairs and verify-cache contents. A missing or malformed runtime config can therefore block `check-approved` before cache completeness is checked, which couples an independent gate to unrelated runtime settings.

The production portion of this module runs through line 754, so it exceeds the repo's 700-line cap for non-test code. Split the composition helpers into smaller modules before merging; otherwise this file is out of policy and harder to review safely.

If one scope reports `HumanEscalationRequired` and another reports `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`, this function still returns only the semantic-failure path because `semantic_failure_count` is checked before `human_escalation_count`. That masks verifier-degradation cases that are supposed to force human escalation and can send the gate down the wrong remediation path.

A cache read failure is converted into a per-pair `cache read failed` message and the command still returns `Ok(CommandOutcome { exit_code: 1 })`. That swallows cache I/O/corruption errors into an approval miss, making broken storage indistinguishable from a legitimately unapproved pair and hiding the underlying infrastructure failure.

`ref_verify_run` always calls `resolve_scope(..., false)`, while `check-approved` calls `resolve_scope(..., true)`. For a track that already has spec + catalogues but is still `InProgress`, `run` verifies only Chain2, but `check-approved` requires All chains. That breaks the planned commit-gate sequence (`ref-verify run && ref-verify check-approved`): Chain1 cache entries can remain missing/stale and there is no CLI path here to refresh them until the track status changes.

`ref_verify_run` always passes `approval_gate = true` into `resolve_scope`, so any track that has both `spec.json` and complete TDDD catalogues is forced to `RefVerifyScopePlan::All`. That bypasses the phase-aware path in `should_run_all_chains` and makes in-progress/type-design runs verify Chain1 as well as Chain2, despite this module's own `resolve_scope(..., false)` behavior for in-progress catalogues returning `Chain2All`. This can fail or spend verifier budget on stale spec→ADR pairs outside the current phase. Reserve `true` for `check-approved`/final-gate behavior and use the normal phase-aware scope for `run`.

The timeout path only kills the direct verifier child, then immediately joins the stdout/stderr reader threads. If the verifier command is a wrapper that spawns another process inheriting stdout/stderr, killing the parent leaves those pipe fds open and `read_to_string` can block forever, so the configured timeout does not reliably bound the ref-verifier run. Run the child in a process group/job and terminate the whole group on timeout, or otherwise ensure inherited pipe handles cannot keep the collectors blocked.

P1 security: the Codex/Gemini verifier paths run untrusted claim/evidence prompts through agent CLIs without an enforced no-tools boundary. `--sandbox read-only` only prevents writes; it still permits model-driven shell/file reads, and the Gemini path has no sandbox/tool restriction here. A prompt-injected spec/catalogue/ADR claim can cause the verifier to inspect local files or environment and return the data as a valid JSON `citation`/`reason`, which can then be persisted to verify-cache. The Claude path explicitly disallows local tools; apply equivalent isolation/sanitized env for Codex/Gemini or reject providers that cannot enforce it.

`ref-verifier` is launched with `--permission-mode dontAsk` and a denylist, but without any `--allowedTools` whitelist. The local Claude reviewer in this repo uses `--allowedTools` specifically because `dontAsk` only denies tools outside the allowed set in standard environments and a permissive host configuration can broaden the surface; here the verifier is supposed to enforce a no-tools boundary, yet this invocation can inherit whatever tools/settings the host allows except the six denied names. That lets the semantic verifier inspect workspace state through any remaining or host-allowed tools instead of judging only the prompt payload, undermining the gate's isolation. Make the runner pass an explicit empty/no-tool allowlist if supported, or otherwise fail closed instead of relying on a partial denylist.

`ref_verify_run` always calls `resolve_scope(..., false)`, so once both `spec.json` and Chain2 catalogues exist on an in-progress track it runs only `Chain2All` and skips Chain1. The T009 contract says standalone/final-gate execution must cover all current pairs, and `ref_verify_check_approved` already enforces the full approval surface via `resolve_scope(..., true)`, so `run && check-approved` can fail even though `run` just completed because Chain1 cache entries were never refreshed. The run path needs a way to select the approval/all-chains surface when used outside the phase-only guard.

`ref_verify_run` always passes `approval_gate = true` into `resolve_scope`, so any track with both `spec.json` and complete TDDD catalogues runs `All` instead of the phase-aware Chain2 path. That violates AC-02/T009’s context-based routing for type-design phase runs and can block or spend verifier budget on Chain1 pairs outside the current phase.

The timeout only wraps waiting for the direct verifier child. If that child exits while a descendant keeps stdout/stderr open, `join_collector` can block forever after `wait_for_process` returns, so `timeout_secs` no longer bounds `ref-verify run`. Kill or reap the process group before joining the pipe readers, or apply the remaining timeout to the collector joins.

`timeout_secs` comes from `agent-profiles.json` as an unconstrained `u64`; this code only rejects zero and then computes `Instant::now() + timeout`. Very large configured values can make `Instant` addition panic instead of returning `RefVerifyError`, which violates the no-panics-in-library-code rule and can crash `sotp ref-verify run` from configuration input. Use `checked_add` and map overflow to a verifier/config error, or validate an upper bound before constructing the deadline.

The success-path coverage for the public `CliApp::dry_run_fix_local` entry point was replaced with a direct `run_dry_fix_codex` test, so regressions in wrapper logic such as agent-profile loading, provider selection, and model fallback are no longer exercised. Keep at least one happy-path test on the public API itself.

The new `type-design`/`--layer` branch in `invocation_context_from_cli` is not covered by any test in this scope. Right now the file only exercises `spec-design` and `standalone`, so a regression in the new CLI-to-context mapping or the required missing-`--layer` error would still pass CI.

These `dry_run_fix_local` tests only assert that execution reaches provider dispatch, which does not distinguish `model: Some(...)` from the profile fallback. A bug that ignored the explicit model override would still pass here, so the new wrapper behavior is not actually verified.

`test_dry_run_fix_local_none_model_falls_back_to_profile_model_and_reaches_dispatch` and `test_dry_run_fix_local_explicit_model_bypasses_profile_model_and_reaches_dispatch` invoke `dry_run_fix_local` without `process_env_lock`, but that path shells out through `SystemGitRepo::discover()` and this module mutates `PATH` in other tests. Under parallel test execution these cases can inherit a fake `git`/PATH from a concurrent test and fail nondeterministically.

`ref_verify_run` gained a new `type-design`/`layer` branch, but the added tests only cover `invocation_context_from_cli` directly. There is no end-to-end test that `CliApp::ref_verify_run` forwards `input.layer` into `RefVerifyScopeResolver` or surfaces the `--layer` missing/invalid errors through the public API, so a regression in the new CLI-to-scope wiring would still pass the suite.

This replacement drops the only end-to-end success assertion for `CliApp::dry_run_fix_local`. The remaining coverage only checks error paths or the lower-level `run_dry_fix_codex` helper, so a regression in the wrapper's profile loading / model fallback / provider dispatch would no longer fail CI.

The new `type-design` wiring is still not covered on a successful path. The current test only proves that a contrived invalid rules file reaches scope resolution; it never verifies that a valid `--layer` flows through `invocation_context_from_cli` into `RefVerifyScopeResolver` and then into a successful `ref_verify_run` execution. That leaves the new public branch unguarded against wiring regressions.

`ref_verify_run` now trusts a caller-supplied `context`/`layer` tuple and has no fallback to derive the invocation surface from the active track or branch. That means any caller that still invokes `sotp ref-verify run --track-id ...` without explicitly setting `--context` will silently resolve to `standalone -> All`, which regresses the previous phase-aware routing and can run the wrong chain on spec/design or type/design tracks.

The new `type-design` happy-path test uses an empty catalogue, so it never exercises `AgentRefVerifierAdapter` or proves that `RefVerifyInvocationContext::TypeDesign` actually routes to the `ref-verifier-chain2` capability. A regression in the real chain2/provider wiring would still pass this suite, leaving the public API's most important branch unverified.

`ref_verify_run` reads the current git branch before translating and validating the new `context`/`layer` CLI inputs. This can mask the required explicit `--context type-design`/missing `--layer` error with a git discovery or detached-HEAD error, which violates the briefing’s requirement that the CLI-argument-to-typed-context conversion fail clearly. Move `invocation_context_from_cli(&input.context, input.layer.as_deref())` ahead of `current_git_branch` so invalid firing-surface arguments are rejected before any repository state probing.

`ref_verify_run` reads `current_git_branch` before it translates/validates `input.context` and `input.layer`. That means the new type-design API paths can return a git-repo error from a temp directory instead of the required `--layer`/`--context` diagnostic, and the new tests at lines 933-1001 cannot reach the intended scope-resolution assertions without a fake git setup. Validate the invocation context first, then read the branch.

The new `dry_run_fix_local` model-selection tests only assert that execution reaches the unsupported-`claude` provider branch. They never observe the model passed to the fixer, so a regression that ignores `input.model` or stops falling back to the profile model would still pass both tests at lines 961 and 1014. Capture the selected model on the codex path if you want this contract covered.

`test_ref_verify_run_type_design_valid_layer_is_forwarded_to_scope_resolver` never fakes the active git branch. `ref_verify_run()` checks `current_git_branch()` before scope resolution, so on a normal checkout this test will fail with `track is not active` and will not exercise the resolver path it claims to cover. Wrap it in `with_fake_track_branch(...)` (or otherwise stub branch discovery) to make the assertion meaningful.

`ref_verify_run` now has a new public `Standalone`/`CommitGate -> All` path, but this module only exercises the `spec-design` happy path and an empty-catalogue `type-design` case. There is no end-to-end cli-composition test that actually drives the default All-scope branch through `RefVerifyScopeResolver` and `make_ref_verifier_process_runner`, so a regression in the new DI wiring or default context handling could still pass the suite.

The new `standalone` success test does not actually exercise the DI path it claims to validate. Its fixture has no `adr_refs` and no TDDD catalogues, so `RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter::load_pairs()` returns zero pairs and `AgentRefVerifierAdapter` / `make_ref_verifier_process_runner()` are never invoked. A regression in the new subprocess wiring would still pass this test, leaving the critical integration path unverified.

This 'standalone -> All' test is still scope-blind: `write_chain1_fixture()` only seeds Chain1 data, so the test will stay green if `standalone` accidentally resolves to Chain1 or never reaches the verifier. Add a fixture that distinguishes All from Chain1, or assert on the resolved scope.

The positive `check-approved` path has the same blind spot: it uses only Chain1 fixtures, so it would not fail if `ref_verify_check_approved()` stopped resolving `CommitGate` to `All`. Add an All/Chain2-sensitive fixture or an assertion on the resolved scope.

P1: `ref_verify_run` validates the repo path and discovers the git root before it validates `--context`/`--layer`. The comment says the opposite, and a bad context or missing layer can now be masked by an unrelated filesystem/git error. Validate `invocation_context_from_cli` first, then do repo discovery.

P1: The new `standalone` integration test only asserts that the Chain2 cache file exists. A regression that maps `standalone` to `Chain2` instead of `All` would still pass, so the default All-scope branch is not actually protected. Assert a Chain1 artifact as well, or this test leaves the main routing regression untested.

Missing `entry_hashes` entries are silently replaced with `ContentHash::from_bytes([0; 32])`. That fabricates a valid-looking but incorrect `entry_hash` in `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` if the adapter/key-derivation ever drifts, which should fail closed instead of persisting corrupted integrity metadata.

The new shared traversal helper is public and now drives both `catalogue_spec_refs` and `catalogue_spec_signals`, but the added tests only cover type entries. There is no direct coverage for trait/function traversal or for a same-short-name cross-section collision, so a regression in `section_key` formatting or section order would reach production unnoticed.

The merge gate now receives per-entry catalogue hashes, but it immediately discards `_entry_hashes` and only checks signal count, positional name, document-level `catalogue_declaration_hash`, and color. That means a committed `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` can contain a stale or fabricated `CatalogueSpecSignal.entry_hash` while the gate still approves, so the semantic Chain ② cache key no longer reflects the current catalogue entry. Compare each signal's `entry_hash()` against the current entry hash map using the same traversal/order and fail closed on missing or mismatched hashes.

`merge_gate.rs` now has 752 lines of production code before `#[cfg(test)]`, which exceeds the repo's 700-line maximum for production modules. This is an architecture violation and should be split into smaller modules.

The new Chain ② per-entry hash block pushes the production portion of `merge_gate.rs` to line 752 before `#[cfg(test)]`, exceeding the repository's 700-line maximum for production modules. This was 690 lines before the change, so the current patch crosses the guardrail; please split the new catalogue-spec integrity/signals logic into a helper module or otherwise reduce this file below the limit.

The cache merge is inverted: `retained` keeps existing entries whose `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` do not match any current production pair, so cache-hit entries are dropped instead of preserved. A mixed hit/miss run will rewrite the cache without the hits, defeating AC-07 and forcing needless re-review on the next run.

Fast-tier evaluation is still fully sequential. `RefVerifyConfig::max_parallelism` is validated and stored, but `execute` never consults it; both fast passes use plain iterator collection (`.iter().map(...).collect()`). That misses the required parallel fast-tier execution / worker-cap behavior and leaves the configuration dead.

Any cache entry whose hashes match a current production pair is treated as a hit regardless of the stored verdict, so a cached `Fail`/`Pending` is never re-checked. The later merge at `639-669` then filters those same entries out of the rewritten artifact and never adds them back, so current cache hits can disappear entirely on the next save. That breaks AC-07 and can let stale failures vanish from the gate.

The return order is backwards for degraded calibration: `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` is emitted before `final_calibration_healthy` is checked. If known-bad detection stays below threshold and a production `Fail` is also confirmed, the degraded verifier's verdict is still trusted instead of escalating to `HumanEscalationRequired`, which contradicts the intended precedence.

As written, the fast/final verifier loops are strictly serial iterator chains, so the interactor never uses `RefVerifyParallelism` and can only issue one model call at a time. That makes the validated `max_parallelism` config dead and violates the requirement that fast-tier review be parallelized per pair.

`save_entries` runs before the final known-bad health check, so a batch that ends in `HumanEscalationRequired` still persists the final-tier verdicts from a degraded verifier into the verify-cache. The ADR/spec text says degraded-verifier verdicts are not trustworthy and should not be cached; gate cache writes on `final_calibration_healthy` (or defer persistence until after this check).
The production portion of this module is 767 lines long before the `#[cfg(test)]` block starts at line 770, which exceeds the repository's 700-line production-module limit.

The new public `RefVerifyApplicationService::execute` path is only covered on success-oriented scenarios; there is no test that stubs `RefVerifyPairSourcePort`, `RefVerifyVerifierPort`, or `RefVerifyCachePort` to return an error and asserts the corresponding fail-closed `RefVerifyError` variant is propagated. That leaves the main error-handling contract for this usecase unverified, which is a P1 coverage gap for a new public API.

The new public error variants `VerifierPort` and `CachePersistence` forward raw adapter `message` strings verbatim into `Display`, so any hostnames, SQL fragments, or stack traces returned by adapters will leak into user-visible output/logs. Redact or normalize these messages at the boundary instead of exposing the raw string.

The interactor test suite only covers success and behavioral cases. There are no tests that make `pair_source.load_pairs`, `cache.load_entries/save_entries`, or `verifier.verify_pair` return `Err`, so the new `VerifierPort` and `CachePersistence` failure paths are unverified. Add negative tests for each port boundary to satisfy the public-API test requirement.

On the first `Err` or panic from a worker, this returns immediately and drops the remaining `JoinHandle`s for the chunk. Those threads keep running detached, so a failed batch can still continue calling the verifier after the use case has already reported failure. Join or cancel the rest of the chunk before returning so the gate actually aborts fail-closed.

`HumanEscalationRequired { pair_count }` undercounts unresolved work when a degraded batch also has pending production pairs. The code returns either `confirmed_pending` or `known_bad_probes.len()`, so a run with both categories reports only one side of the problem instead of the total unresolved count described by the type docs.

`RefVerifyError::TrackNotActive` advertises an active-track guard, but `RefVerifyCommand` carries no current-branch/active-track input and this usecase layer has no branch-reader port. As written, nothing in this scope can actually enforce the promised guard, so the public API can run on a non-track branch without surfacing `TrackNotActive`.

The result classification gives `SemanticFailuresConfirmed` precedence over `HumanEscalationRequired`. If a batch contains both a confirmed production fail and at least one unresolved/pending pair, the function returns the fail variant and masks the pending case, even though the type docs say pending/unresolved results must surface as human escalation. This needs pending to win or a split result path.

`Pending` is checked before `Fail`, so a healthy batch that contains both verdicts returns `HumanEscalationRequired` instead of `SemanticFailuresConfirmed`. The intended rule is to prioritize confirmed failures in this mixed case so the writer/fix loop can act on them.

Cache retention is global across scopes: `fresh_hashes` is built without `cache_scope`, and the retain filter drops any existing entry whose `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` appears in any freshly evaluated pair. If the same hash pair exists in another scope, saving this run will delete that scope's cache entry even though it was never re-evaluated. The pruning needs to be scope-local because caches are stored in separate artifacts per scope.

The D12 skip path handles cached `SemanticVerdict::Pending`, but the added tests only cover all-Pass cache hits and cached Fail. The briefing explicitly requires cached Pending on the skip path to surface as `HumanEscalationRequired`; add a test with a cached Pending entry that asserts `HumanEscalationRequired` and no verifier calls, so this fail-closed behavior cannot regress unnoticed.

The new public `sotp ref-verify` entry point is only exercised through a local `TestCli` wrapper around `RefVerifyCommand`, so the tests do not prove that the top-level binary actually exposes `ref-verify` or that `run_cli` dispatches `CliCommand::RefVerify` to the new handler. A typo or missing top-level wiring here would still pass the new tests, leaving the public CLI API uncovered. Add top-level parse/dispatch coverage for `sotp ref-verify run` and `sotp ref-verify check-approved`, including an error case, consistent with the repository's public API test requirement.

`prompt_template_path` points at `.harness/prompts/ref-verifier.md`, but that template file does not exist in the repository. Any code path that tries to render the ref-verifier prompt will fail closed at runtime, so the new capability is not actually usable yet.

The prompt contradicts its own fail criteria: after saying unrelated, contradictory, or over-broad claims should return `fail`, it then says that if the verifier cannot quote supporting text it should return `pending`. For the common deterministic-failure case where evidence does not support the claim, there is no supporting quote, so the model can classify real semantic mismatches as `pending` instead of `fail`. That routes fixable reference errors into the human-escalation path rather than the writer/fix loop, weakening the semantic gate.

Staging happens before the new semantic gate runs, but `bin/sotp ref-verify run` writes `spec-adr-verify-cache.json` and `<layer>-catalogue-spec-verify-cache.json` under `track/items/<track-id>`. Those files are created after `git add-all`, so the commit can omit the cache artifacts that the gate just produced unless you restage or move staging to the end.

This describes the commit path as an all-chain semantic gate, but the wrapper only shells out to `bin/sotp ref-verify run` with no explicit all-chain mode. In the current ref-verify flow that command is context-scoped, so an in-progress track can still skip Chain1; the gate can therefore pass with stale or missing Chain1 semantic cache.

`ref-verify run` creates/updates verify-cache artifacts under `track/items/<track-id>/`, and those files are not listed in `review_operational` exclusions. This task now runs `review check-approved` before generating those artifacts and only stages them afterward via `bin/sotp git add-all`, so the code-review gate can pass on a pre-generation diff and then commit newly generated, unreviewed/stale-hash files. Move the review approval check after the ref-verify generation/staging step, or exclude verify-cache artifacts from review hashing if they are intended to be operational output.

`track-commit-message` now runs `bin/sotp git add-all` only after `ci`, code review, semantic ref verification, and DRY approval have all passed. That helper stages the whole worktree except transient `tmp` paths, not just the verify-cache artifacts, so the command validates one snapshot and then commits a later broadly staged snapshot. Any source/doc change created after the gates run is included by the following commit without CI/review/DRY/ref-verify approval. Keep the initial broad staging before the gates and stage only the generated verify-cache paths after `ref-verify run`, or rerun all gates after the final broad staging.

`ref-verify run` is executed after `bin/sotp git add-all`, but that command updates tracked verify-cache artifacts under `track/items/<track-id>/` while `ref-verify check-approved` reads the worktree. The gate can pass on freshly generated cache entries that are never staged, so the following commit can omit the semantic approval artifacts it just relied on. Restage the generated verify-cache files after this step, or move staging after the semantic gate without allowing unverified broad worktree changes into the commit.

The second broad `bin/sotp git add-all` runs after `cargo make ci` and `bin/sotp review check-approved`, so any file changed or created during `ref-verify run` gets staged into the commit without those earlier gates covering it. This weakens the strict commit gate; restage only the ref-verify cache artifacts, or rerun the required gates after restaging.

P1: Moving the broad `bin/sotp git add-all` to after `cargo make ci`, `review check-approved`, `ref-verify check-approved`, and `dry check-approved` means the task validates one snapshot and then stages the whole worktree for commit afterward. Any source/doc change created or modified after the gates run, including artifacts emitted by `ref-verify run`, can be committed without those gates covering the final staged contents. Keep the initial broad staging before the gates and only restage the specific ref-verify cache artifacts afterward, or rerun the gates after the final broad staging.

The commit wrapper now restages the whole worktree after `review check-approved` has already passed. `bin/sotp ref-verify run --context commit-gate` writes verification/cache artifacts, and the following `bin/sotp git add-all` can add those files, or any other concurrent working-tree changes, into the final commit without re-running `bin/sotp review check-approved` on the final staged index. The strict review gate therefore no longer covers exactly what gets committed, even though the workflow docs claim review approval is part of the commit gate's AND evaluation. Keep the committed index fixed after review approval, or rerun review approval after the post-ref-verify staging step.

`track-commit-message` runs `cargo make ci` before the new post-ref-verify `bin/sotp git add-all`, and that helper stages the whole worktree except `tmp`. Any source/config/doc changes produced during `ref-verify run` or otherwise present before the second broad stage can now enter the final commit without CI covering the committed snapshot. Rerun CI after the final broad stage, or stage only the expected verify-cache artifacts instead of broad-staging again.

`track-commit-message` validates the tree with CI, review, ref-verify, and DRY before running the only broad `bin/sotp git add-all`. Since `git add-all` stages the whole worktree except transient `tmp` paths, the final commit can include files created or changed after those gates ran, without rerunning the gates on the committed snapshot. Keep a fixed staged snapshot through the gates, or only stage the expected generated verify-cache artifacts and rerun any required checks that must cover them.

`track-commit-message` runs `bin/sotp ref-verify run --context commit-gate` before staging, CI, and the existing `review check-approved` gate. The T010 briefing requires adding the ref-verify run/check pair after `bin/sotp review check-approved` and before `bin/sotp dry check-approved` so the existing code-review gate remains the first approval gate in that chain; the new workflow text also lists review approval before semantic verification. As written, a commit attempt that is going to be rejected by code review can still invoke the semantic reviewer and update cache artifacts first. Move the `ref-verify run` invocation next to `ref-verify check-approved` after `review check-approved` and before the DRY gate.

D7 states that concrete model values should live in `agent-profiles.json` and that non-canonical configuration examples should not be placed in the ADR body, but the preceding paragraph includes a concrete provider/model example (`claude-opus-4-7`, `gpt-5.4-mini`). Remove the concrete example or relax the stated rule; as written the ADR contradicts itself on whether model examples belong in the ADR.

T001 says to parse ADR front-matter with `serde_yaml::Value` and manually walk `decisions[].id`. That conflicts with the typed-deserialization rule for `libs/infrastructure/src/verify/`, which requires typed `Deserialize` DTOs instead of untyped Value walking in verify/guard code.

T003 makes `CatalogueSpecSignal.entry_hash` a required `ContentHash`, but T005 still requires decoding legacy `<layer>-catalogue-spec-signals.json` files without `entry_hash` and returning a `None`-equivalent. Those requirements cannot both be true as written; the codec needs either an explicit optional/migration wrapper or a non-optional field with a different legacy-handling rule.

T008 still permits a `todo!()`-style stub in `libs/infrastructure/src/ref_verify.rs`. That is a panic path in library code, which conflicts with the repo's no-panics rule and makes the adapter path unsafe if the stub is ever exercised.

The `RoundType` doc says `RefVerifierPort.verify_pair` takes `RoundType`, but `usecase-types.json` defines that port parameter as `domain::tddd::ModelTier`, and T008 explicitly says the adapter performs the `ModelTier -> RoundType` conversion. This catalogue entry is therefore stale and contradicts the layer boundary.

T008 makes the only planned `RefVerifierPort` implementation fail-closed after resolving provider/model, deferring the actual model invocation to a future stub. That contradicts the same plan's `ref-verify run`, cache-writing, and `check-approved` acceptance path, because no implementation task can ever produce `Pass` entries for the verify-cache artifacts.

`RefVerifierPort::verify_pair` returns a full `SemanticVerifyEntry` containing `claim_hash` and `evidence_hash`, but the port receives only raw `claim` and `evidence` strings plus a tier. IN-04 defines those hashes as externally determined identities, especially ADR git blob hash for Chain①, so the adapter cannot construct the required cache key correctly from this interface.

`CatalogueSpecSignal` is declared with a complete field list containing only `type_name`, `signal`, and `entry_hash`, but the spec and plan require the existing `catalogue_declaration_hash` to be retained and coexist with the new per-entry hash. Implementing this catalogue literally would remove the whole-file hash that CN-10 says must remain.

T007 requires the usecase layer to downgrade a citation-less Pass to Pending and even test that case, but the same artifacts make that state unrepresentable: `SemanticVerdict::Pass` requires `EvidenceCitation`, and `RefVerifierPort` returns `SemanticVerdict`. A citation-less pass cannot reach the interactor; this handling belongs in the adapter/codec boundary or should be removed from the usecase task.

T007 assigns `VerifySemanticRefsInteractor` responsibility for enumerating Chain pairs, reading existing verify-cache artifacts, skipping unchanged `(claim_hash, evidence_hash)` pairs, and writing updated cache artifacts, but the usecase catalogue only injects `Arc<dyn RefVerifierPort>`. There is no secondary port for spec/catalogue reads, cache persistence, hash source, or ref-verify config, so the plan either forces infrastructure I/O into the usecase layer or leaves the interactor unable to perform its stated workflow. Add explicit usecase ports/dependencies for these external operations or move the I/O responsibility out of the interactor plan.

The infrastructure catalogue declares `AgentRefVerifierAdapter::new` with only `profiles: Arc<AgentProfiles>`, while T008 says the adapter stores an agent execution client/runner and tests require `AgentRefVerifierAdapter::new` to receive both `AgentProfiles` and the runner. As written, the catalogue and implementation plan disagree on the adapter boundary, making the planned tests/implementation inconsistent. Include the runner/client dependency in the type catalogue constructor or revise T008 to explain how invocation is supplied without injection.

`RefVerifyError` documents `EscalationRequired` as human escalation, but the spec explicitly excludes immediate human escalation for known-bad-pair degradation and requires the D5 path to try the heavyweight model first before reporting to a human. T007 also says a below-threshold known-error detection test returns `EscalationRequired`, so an implementer following the catalogue would short-circuit the required段2 escalation path. Rename/scope the error to final human escalation only, or add a separate model-escalation state that preserves the D5 sequence.
